key_points: Gravitation is the force of attraction between any two objects in the universe
Sir Isaac Newton proposed the universal law of gravitation
According to the law, gravitational force depends on the masses of the objects and the distance between them
Free fall is the motion of an object under the influence of gravity alone
Acceleration due to gravity (g) near the Earth's surface is approximately 9.8 m/s²
Mass is the amount of matter in an object and remains constant everywhere
Weight is the force with which the Earth attracts an object and depends on gravity
Pressure is defined as force acting per unit area
Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid on an immersed object
Archimedes' principle explains the buoyant force acting on objects in fluids
examples: Object falling from a tree due to gravity
Moon revolving around the Earth due to gravitational attraction
Weight of a person being less on the Moon than on Earth
Ships floating on water due to buoyancy
Submarines controlling depth using buoyant force
Hydrometer used to measure density of liquids
formulas: Universal law of gravitation → F = G(Mm)/r²
Weight of an object → W = m × g
Acceleration due to gravity → g = GM / R²
exam_tips: Remember that gravitational force acts between all masses in the universe
Mass remains constant but weight changes with gravity
Acceleration due to gravity on Earth is about 9.8 m/s²
Objects float when buoyant force equals or exceeds their weight
Archimedes' principle is important for understanding floating and sinking
